diagrams.title: Python Package Directory Structure
diagrams.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the structure of a Python package for IoT applications
elements: Arrows showing: parent-child relationships between packages
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how packages are organized in directory hierarchies with __init__.py files, enabling them to structure their IoT project code properly for maintainability and reusability

diagrams.title: Package Installation and Import Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing the process from installing a package to importing it in Python code
elements: Step 2: Package downloaded from PyPI to site-packages/
Step 3: Python interpreter searches in sys.path
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete lifecycle of how third-party packages move from installation (pip) to being available for import in Python scripts, which is essential for using IoT libraries like paho-mqtt, RPi.GPIO, and requests.

diagrams.title: Virtual Environment Architecture
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing isolated virtual environments for different IoT projects
elements: Virtual Env 1: weather_app/ with specific package versions
Virtual Env 2: home_automation/ with different package versions
Isolation arrows showing no cross-contamination between venvs
Benefit: Different projects can use different package versions
diagrams.purpose: Students learn why virtual environments are critical for IoT development, allowing them to manage dependencies for different projects without version conflicts—essential when working with multiple IoT devices and applications.

diagrams.title: Creating Custom Package Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flowchart for creating and distributing a custom IoT package
elements: Step boxes: 1) Create directory structure, 2) Add __init__.py, 3) Write modules, 4) Create setup.py or pyproject.toml, 5) Test locally with pip install -e ., 6) Upload to PyPI
Output: Package available for pip install
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete workflow to create their own Python packages, enabling them to organize and distribute reusable IoT code across projects or teams.

charts.title: Module vs Package in Python
charts.content: Comparison showing: Module = single .py file, Package = directory with __init__.py containing modules; Import syntax differences; Examples; Use cases for IoT development

charts.title: pip Commands for IoT Developers
charts.content: Table with columns: Command, Purpose, IoT Example; Rows: pip install, pip list, pip freeze, pip uninstall, pip show; Purpose: Quick reference for package management commands used in IoT projects

charts.title: Package Selection Decision Tree
charts.content: Flowchart helping IoT developers choose right package: Start → What type? → Hardware (RPi.GPIO, smbus) / Communication (paho-mqtt, requests) / Data (numpy, pandas) → Specific package recommendation; Includes consideration for device constraints (Raspberry Pi vs ESP32)

charts.title: Popular IoT Python Packages Categories
charts.content: Table with columns: Category, Package Names, Purpose; Rows: Hardware (RPi.GPIO, smbus2, adafruit-blinka), Communication (paho-mqtt, requests, urllib3), Cloud/AWS (boto3, aws-iot-device-sdk), Data Processing (numpy, pandas)

charts.title: Package File Structure Checklist
charts.content: Reference table listing required and optional files: __init__.py (required), __main__.py (optional for executable), setup.py/pyproject.toml (for distribution), README.md, LICENSE, requirements.txt with descriptions of each file's role

branches: Package Fundamentals: Directory structure, __init__.py, __all__, relative imports
Package Installation: pip, PyPI, pip install paho-mqtt RPi.GPIO, pip list, pip freeze
Virtual Environments: venv, virtualenv, isolation, requirements.txt
Creating Packages: setup.py, pyproject.toml, wheel, source distribution
IoT-Specific Packages: Hardware control, Cloud connectivity, Data analytics
Best Practices: Version management, dependencies, virtual environments
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of Python packages relevant to IoT development, showing the ecosystem from basic concepts to specialized IoT libraries, helping students understand how package knowledge enables building production IoT applications
